source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 8.6 File attributes (pp. 150-153)

questions.q: For a FILE, what do r, w and x permit?
options: r = read/copy the file, w = modify its content, x = execute it
r = rename, w = read, x = delete
r = run, w = read, x = write
r = read the directory, w = enter it, x = list it
questions.why: On a file: r reads/copies, w modifies content, x runs it as a program/script.

questions.q: For a DIRECTORY, what does the x permission allow?
options: Entering the directory (cd into it)
Executing the directory as a program
Deleting the directory
Reading the directory listing
questions.why: On a directory: r lists its contents, w creates/deletes entries in it, and x lets you cd into it (r and x usually go together).

questions.q: What do the user classes u, g and o stand for?
options: u = owner (user), g = owning group, o = other users
u = users, g = guests, o = owners
u = universal, g = global, o = organisation
u = update, g = grant, o = override
questions.why: u is the owner, g the owning group, o everyone else; 'a' (all) is shorthand for ugo.

questions.q: In octal permissions, r=4, w=2, x=1. What does 755 represent?
options: rwxr-xr-x
rw-r--r--
rwxrwxrwx
r-xr-xr-x
questions.why: 7 = 4+2+1 = rwx for the owner; 5 = 4+1 = r-x for group and others, giving rwxr-xr-x.

questions.q: Using octal notation, set the permissions of file1 to rwxr----x (owner rwx, group read, others execute).
questions.why: owner rwx = 7, group r-- = 4, others --x = 1, so chmod 741.

questions.q: Give the OWNER execute permission on script.sh using symbolic notation (leave everything else unchanged).
questions.why: Symbolic form is [ugoa][+-=][rwx]; u+x adds execute for the owner only.

questions.q: Who is allowed to change the permissions of a file?
options: The administrator and the file's owner
Any user on the system
Only the administrator
Only members of the owning group
questions.why: Only root and the file's owner can change its permissions.

questions.q: In 'chmod 4755 file', what does the leading 4 do?
options: It sets a special permission (the set-uid bit)
It sets read permission for others
It is ignored
It makes the file read-only
questions.why: A fourth leading octal digit sets special permissions; 4 here is set-uid (a topic covered more fully later).
